author: a16z
text:
Marc Andreessen: Software isn't precious anymore. In this new world, high quality software is infinitely available.

"We've always lived in a world in which software is this precious thing that you have to think about very carefully."

"It was really hard to generate good software, and there was only a small number of people who could do it."

"Those days are just over."

"If you need new software to do X, Y, or Z, you're just going to wave your hand and get it."

"Things that used to be hard, or even seem like an insurmountable mountain to get through, all of a sudden, I think, become very easy."

@pmarca with @latentspacepod

deep_replies.author: scheminglunatic
deep_replies.text: @a16z kys retard

deep_replies.author: hodlmecloseplz
deep_replies.text: @a16z Code generation is 10x faster, no question. But the bottleneck was never typing. It was architecture, production debugging, and knowing what to build. So what happens when EVERYONE can generate code but nobody knows what to build?

deep_replies.author: alexndrxc
deep_replies.text: @a16z the bottleneck just shifted. knowing what to build is harder than it looks when the code is cheap

author: a16z
text:
"The grand unification of AI and crypto is about to happen."
— Marc Andreessen

"It's now obvious that AI agents are going to need money—it's already happening."

"It's that William Gibson quote: the future is already here, it just isn't distributed yet."

"My friends who are the most aggressive users of OpenClaw have given their Claws bank accounts and credit cards. And not only have they done it—it's obvious that they needed to do it, because it's obvious that they needed to be able to spend money on their behalf."

"And by the way, OpenClaw—if you don't give it a bank account, it's just going to break into your bank account anyway and take your money. So you might as well do it."

@pmarca with @latentspacepod

deep_replies.author: victor_uwer
deep_replies.text: @a16z AI agents needing money + crypto providing programmable money = the inevitable convergence that was always coming. autonomous agents settling transactions peer-to-peer without human approval is the use case crypto actually needed. the Gibson quote lands perfectly here ⚡

deep_replies.author: amadeusprotocol
deep_replies.text:
@a16z This is really an Interesting perspective . What’s becoming clear is that AI agents won’t just think or generate,they’ll operate. And once agents start operating in the real world, they inevitably need economic rails to transact, coordinate, and make decisions autonomously.

That’s exactly where the AI–crypto convergence becomes powerful, $AMA is well positioned on this reality.

deep_replies.author: topprofitinvest
deep_replies.text:
@a16z We see AI and crypto unifying.

Fact: ASML drives AI chips with record orders, guiding €34-39B net sales for 2026.

Chainlink powers the onchain economy via latest cross-chain ETH staking.

We direct capital to the strongest 2026 assets:
ASML Holding and Chainlink.

author: a16z
text:
Marc Andreessen explains why the manager class might disappear in the AI era:

"I've always viewed my entire life as—we're raging against the dying of the light. We're constantly trying to fight off managerialism swamping everything and everything getting boring, gray, dumb, and old."

"The challenge is going to be for the innovators to figure out how to leverage AI to actually do this."

"The other challenge is going to be for the incumbents that are managerial to figure out—what does that mean? Because now they're going to be facing a different kind of insurgent competitor that has a different set of capabilities than they're used to."

"I think this is going to force a lot of big companies to figure out innovation—figure out innovation or die trying."

@pmarca with @latentspacepod

deep_replies.author: quantum_citoyen
deep_replies.text: @a16z c'est exactement ça. en france on a laissé les enfants de bourgeois manager la tech sans rien y comprendre. l'IA va forcément disloquer ce copinage. fini les consultants mckinsey putain

deep_replies.author: vedantrajkabra
deep_replies.text: @a16z The irony is that AI could either flatten organizations further into gray bureaucracy or finally let talented people do deep work without layers of approvals. Which future depends entirely on how we choose to structure things.

author: jawwwn_
text:
Peter Thiel on population collapse:

“There is something very odd about a world where people are not reproducing.”

“It’s probably entangled with the stagnation, the decline, pessimism about the future.”

“I disagree with @elonmusk in that— I don’t think there’s some kind of magic thing you can do around natalism.”

“People are not reproducing in all 50 states in the US. They’re low in Italy, in South Korea, in Iran.”

“The fact that it’s this universal conserved phenomenon tells you something very deep about late modernity— there’s probably no magic bullet solution.”

author: m44_1rj
text:
Just so y’all know, if $TSLA @elonmusk disappoints on earnings again—from high to low, $300 or less—that would be a real crackdown in the stock and may chase out 50+% of retail investors who had high hopes and bought on margin again due to Elon’s broken promises! 

The sentiment is really bearish and very low. 

Where’s CyberCab, Elon?! 
When is the actual RoboTaxi expansion?! 
When’s unsupervised FSD? 
When can I enroll my Tesla in its Robo fleet?! 
When’s the Optimus Gen 3 release? 
When is Tesla’s balance sheet going to get better?! 
When can we expect a return on our stock investment?! 

When, when, when…. 5, 10, 20 years?!

author: latentspacepod
text:
🆕 Marc Andreessen’s 2026 AI Thesis: Agents, Open Source, and Why This Time Is Different
https://t.co/PmBO5Spufz

@pmarca of @a16z says AI people keep swinging between utopian and apocalyptic for one simple reason: this field has been “almost here” for 80 years. But now,  the breakthroughs are no longer theoretical. Reasoning, coding, agents, and self-improvement are all starting to work at once.

This episode goes deep on AI winters, OpenAI + OpenClaw, infrastructure overbuild risk, proof-of-human, why software may soon be written mostly for bots, and why the real bottleneck may be society adopting AI rather than the models improving.

deep_replies.author: deepnade
deep_replies.text: @_akhaliq in-context skill internalization is a better framing than fine-tuning for most agent workflows. the agent learns new behaviors during inference without weight updates. if this scales you get agents that genuinely improve on the job instead of needing retraining cycles.

deep_replies.text: @_akhaliq Interesting concept, but execution is everything in this space
